text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Automation Bias and Over-Trust lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Automation bias occurs when people accept AI outputs too readily because they appear confident, precise, or authoritative.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Automation bias occurs when people accept AI outputs too readily because they appear confident, precise, or authoritative.
textPreview: Automation bias occurs when people accept AI outputs too readily because they appear confident, precise, or authoritative.

text: Automation bias is a practical operational risk. Staff may over-trust outputs that are fluent, precise, or repeated often. Public health workflows need review steps that make skepticism normal and expected.
textPreview: Automation bias is a practical operational risk. Staff may over-trust outputs that are fluent, precise, or repeated often. Public health ...

text: Repeated exposure to fluent AI outputs can reduce scrutiny unless workflows require deliberate review and documentation.
textPreview: Repeated exposure to fluent AI outputs can reduce scrutiny unless workflows require deliberate review and documentation.

text:
Use this slide in the Automation Bias and Over-Trust training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 14, 40, 46, 6.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Repeated exposure to fluent AI outputs can reduce scrutiny unless workflows require deliberate review and documentation.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Automation Bias and Over-Trust training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 14, 40, 46, 6.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Automation bias occurs when people give too much weight to AI output because it appears confident, precise, or authoritative. In public health, this can affect surveillance review, risk scoring, inspection documentation, prioritization, public messaging, or resource decisions. Deliberate review practices are needed so AI supports, rather than substitutes for, professional judgment.

text: Definition: Automation bias occurs when people over-rely on AI outputs because the system appears fast, confident, technical, or authoritative. Over-trust can be especially risky in high-pressure public health settings.
textPreview: Definition: Automation bias occurs when people over-rely on AI outputs because the system appears fast, confident, technical, or authorit...

Use this slide in the Automation Bias and Over-Trust training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 14, 40, 46, 6.
Reinforce this definition: Definition: Automation bias occurs when people over-rely on AI outputs because the system appears fast, confident, technical, or authoritative. Over-trust can be especially risky in high-pressure public health settings.

Staff stop checking sources because AI summaries are usually fluent. Risk scores become the primary basis for decisions without review of context. Draft messages are approved without checking public health guidance, equity implications, or.

text: Use second-review requirements for high-impact outputs. Require confidence, limitation, source, and uncertainty notes in briefs. Separate AI recommendations from human decisions in documentation. Train staff to look for omissions, unsupported.

text: Audit samples of AI-assisted work. Track overrides, corrections, incidents, complaints, and false positives or false negatives. Review whether staff are using the tool as intended or developing unsafe shortcuts.

Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Warning Signs, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Automation Bias and Over-Trust training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 14, 40, 46, 6.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Where might staff accept AI output too quickly? What second-review or sign-off is needed for high-impact outputs? How will overrides, corrections, and disagreements be documented?

text: Take one AI-assisted output and design a review form that asks staff to check sources, uncertainty, missing context, subgroup impact, and whether the recommendation should be accepted, modified, or rejected.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Automation bias review checklist, Decision owner sign-off process, Override and correction log.
